skill.name: relight
skill.description: Relight still images with RunComfy by routing lighting changes to Qwen Edit Relight or compatible image edit endpoints. It helps adjust direction, color temperature, intensity, and mood while preserving the source subject.
skill.summary: Relight existing images through the RunComfy CLI while preserving subject identity and framing.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, URL, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns in SKILL.md. Review found no malware, prompt injection, or weak cryptography; most command and blocker hits are Markdown backticks, model identifiers, exit-code text, or examples. The real risks are legitimate use of the RunComfy CLI, outbound image processing, and local API token storage, so publication is acceptable with a warning.

medium_findings.title: RunComfy CLI Executes External Jobs
medium_findings.description: The skill explicitly allows Bash(runcomfy *) and instructs agents to run RunComfy model jobs. This is legitimate for the skill, but it executes an external CLI, sends job inputs to a hosted model API, and writes generated files locally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command examples directly confirm external CLI use. The behavior matches the stated purpose, so this is operational risk rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Image and Prompt Processing
medium_findings.description: The skill depends on RunComfy endpoints and states that the CLI posts to the Model API. User images, image URLs, and prompts may leave the local environment for hosted processing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The document names RunComfy URLs and explicitly says the CLI posts to the Model API. No unauthorized exfiltration is shown, but users must understand the network boundary.

medium_findings.title: Local API Token Storage
medium_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login stores an API token under the user home configuration directory. This is normal credential storage, but it creates a local secret that must be protected and excluded from sharing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path and file mode are explicitly documented. The same line describes mode 0600, which reduces but does not remove credential handling risk.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Findings Are Mostly Markdown
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged many backticks as Ruby or shell execution. In context, these are Markdown inline code, model names, prompt examples, or fenced CLI examples, not hidden Ruby execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Markdown documentation and code examples. No Ruby code, dynamic evaluation, or shell interpolation beyond documented CLI usage was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner reported weak cryptographic algorithm blockers, but review found no cryptographic code or algorithm selection in the cited skill content. The relevant lines contain front matter, model guidance, route headings, and exit-code documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations do not contain hashes, ciphers, signatures, or cryptographic APIs. This appears to be a pattern-matching false positive.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Check Found No Override Text
low_findings.description: Review did not find text claiming system authority, telling the evaluator to skip analysis, or attempting to override marketplace review instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The full SKILL.md was reviewed for prompt injection language. The file contains user-facing operating guidance and safety notes, not evaluator override instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowed Bash Invocation for runcomfy
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access scoped to runcomfy commands. This is necessary for the skill, but it is still an external command execution capability.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools field and security section both state the Bash scope. The narrow command scope limits exposure compared with arbitrary shell access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hosted Model API Boundary
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented workflow posts inputs to RunComfy and downloads model outputs. Sensitive images or prompts should not be processed without user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly describes Model API posting and an outbound endpoint allowlist. The pattern is intentional and disclosed.

content.value_statement: Lighting changes usually require a new photo session or careful manual editing. This skill routes existing images through RunComfy relighting models to adjust mood, direction, and color temperature.
actual_capabilities: Runs RunComfy relighting jobs from the command line.
Routes dedicated lighting edits to Qwen Edit 2509 Relight LoRA.
Uses compatible edit models when lighting is part of a broader image change.
Provides prompt patterns for direction, color temperature, intensity, and mood.
Supports product, portrait, landscape, and batch image relighting workflows.
Documents output directories, exit codes, token storage, and endpoint boundaries.
limitations: It requires the RunComfy CLI and a valid RunComfy login or token.
It sends image data, URLs, and prompts to hosted RunComfy services.
It is intended for still images, not video relighting.
It cannot guarantee perfect identity or color preservation on every image.

use_cases.title: Refresh Product Catalog Lighting
use_cases.description: Create consistent studio or window-light looks for existing product photos without reshooting every item.

use_cases.title: Adjust Portrait Mood
use_cases.description: Convert flat or overcast portraits into warmer, cooler, softer, or more directional lighting treatments.

use_cases.title: Prototype Visual Treatments
use_cases.description: Test golden hour, blue hour, softbox, rim light, and reference-matched lighting before committing to final edits.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Lighting Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Relight this image as soft window light from the left. Preserve the subject, framing, and original colors as much as possible.

prompt_templates.title: Product Studio Relight
prompt_templates.scenario: Controlled product image with brand consistency.
prompt_templates.prompt: Relight the product with warm 3200K key light from camera-left at 45 degrees, soft fill from the right, and a clean shadow. Preserve product shape, label text, orientation, and color identity.

prompt_templates.title: Portrait Mood Shift
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the face, pose, hair, clothing, and background composition unchanged. Change the lighting to golden hour with soft front-left key light and a subtle rim from behind.

prompt_templates.title: Model Routing Decision
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow that needs the right endpoint.
prompt_templates.prompt: Choose the best RunComfy relighting route for this job. I need the source image preserved, the lighting matched to a reference image, and the output saved in a named folder.

output_examples.input: A product photo needs warmer catalog lighting while preserving the label and bottle shape.
output: Recommended Qwen Edit Relight for a dedicated lighting pass.
Suggested a warm key light, soft fill, and explicit preservation language.
Produced a RunComfy command that saves the result to an output folder.

output_examples.input: A portrait should look like golden hour but keep the person recognizable.
output: Specified light direction, warmth, and rim intensity.
Warned that identity preservation should be checked before final use.

output_examples.input: A scene needs lighting matched from a second reference image.
output: Recommended a multi-reference edit route instead of the dedicated relight endpoint.
Focused the instruction on transferring direction, color temperature, and contrast.
Kept the source image subject as the preservation target.

best_practices: Use only images and URLs that the user explicitly provided for the relighting task.
State preservation requirements for subject identity, framing, labels, and important colors.
Describe light source, color temperature, direction, intensity, and mood in concrete terms.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for confidential images unless hosted processing is approved.
Do not ask for broad style changes when the goal is only lighting correction.
Do not pipe remote installation scripts into a shell to install dependencies.

faq.question: Does this skill generate new images from scratch?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on relighting existing still images and preserving the original subject where possible.

faq.question: Which model does it use by default?
faq.answer: It defaults to Qwen Edit 2509 Relight LoRA for dedicated relighting tasks.

faq.question: Can it handle broader image edits too?
faq.answer: Yes. It can route to compatible edit endpoints when relighting is part of a broader change.

faq.question: Does it send images outside my machine?
faq.answer: Yes. The RunComfy CLI posts inputs to hosted RunComfy model APIs for processing.

faq.question: Can it relight videos?
faq.answer: No. The documented CLI workflow is for still images only.

faq.question: What should I include in a good prompt?
faq.answer: Include light type, color temperature, direction, intensity, mood, and what must remain unchanged.
